codeSnapshot.label: perplexity_eval.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import numpy as np
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

# Perplexity = exp(-(1/N) Σ log p(wₜ|w₁,...,wₜ₋₁))
# Lower perplexity = model is less surprised = better language model

def compute_perplexity(model, tokenizer, text: str,
                       stride: int = 512, max_length: int = 1024) -> float:
    """
    Sliding-window perplexity for long texts.
    Uses stride to avoid conditioning on truncated context.
    """
    encodings = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt')
    input_ids = encodings['input_ids']          # (1, T)
    T = input_ids.shape[1]

    nlls = []  # negative log-likelihoods per token
    prev_end_loc = 0
    for begin_loc in range(0, T, stride):
        end_loc = min(begin_loc + max_length, T)
        trg_len  = end_loc - prev_end_loc    # tokens to score in this window
        input_chunk = input_ids[:, begin_loc:end_loc].to(model.device)
        target_ids  = input_chunk.clone()
        # Mask context tokens (don't score them — they're conditioning context)
        target_ids[:, :-trg_len] = -100

        with torch.no_grad():
            outputs = model(input_chunk, labels=target_ids)
            # loss = mean negative log-likelihood over non-masked positions
            nll = outputs.loss * trg_len  # total NLL for this window's tokens
        nlls.append(nll)
        prev_end_loc = end_loc
        if end_loc == T:
            break

    # Perplexity = exp(mean NLL per token)
    ppl = torch.exp(torch.stack(nlls).sum() / T).item()
    return ppl

# Example: compute perplexity of GPT-2 on a sentence
model_name = 'gpt2'
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)
model.eval()

text = 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.'
ppl = compute_perplexity(model, tokenizer, text)
print(f'Perplexity: {ppl:.2f}')
# GPT-2 on Penn Treebank: ~29.4
# LLaMA-7B on Penn Treebank: ~5.7
# Uniform random over 50K vocab: ~50,000

eli3: Perplexity measures how surprised a language model is by a text. If the model correctly predicts each word with high probability, perplexity is low. If it's frequently shocked by what comes next, perplexity is high.

Think of it like a test: show a language model text one word at a time and ask it to predict the next word. Perplexity is roughly the average number of equally likely choices the model was considering at each step. Perplexity=100 means the model was as confused as if it had to choose randomly from 100 options. Perplexity=3 means it was nearly sure, down to about 3 choices.
Lower perplexity is better. GPT-2 has perplexity ~29 on Penn Treebank; LLaMA-7B has ~5.7. A model that randomly guesses from a 50,000-word vocabulary has perplexity~50,000 — perfectly useless.

whenWhy.title: Comparing language models on the same tokenizer/dataset
whenWhy.body: Perplexity is the standard intrinsic evaluation metric for language models. Lower perplexity on a held-out test set indicates the model better captures the statistical patterns in that domain. Used to compare: different model sizes (7B vs 13B), different architectures (Transformer vs Mamba), or training data quality. Critical caveat: perplexity is only comparable across models using the same tokenizer and test set. Different tokenizers produce different token counts per word, changing the denominator N.

whenWhy.title: Monitoring training progress
whenWhy.body: Validation perplexity is the primary training progress metric for language model pre-training. Decreasing validation perplexity indicates the model is improving. Sudden perplexity spikes indicate gradient instability or data quality issues. Loss = log(perplexity) — for causal LMs, the training objective (cross-entropy loss) is directly interpretable as the log-perplexity. GPT-3 training logs show smooth perplexity decrease over training tokens, allowing extrapolation of final performance.

whenWhy.title: Domain adaptation and fine-tuning evaluation
whenWhy.body: Perplexity measures how well a model has adapted to a new domain. Fine-tuning a general LLM on medical text: perplexity on medical test sentences should decrease (model better predicts medical vocabulary and structure). Catastrophic forgetting: perplexity on general text should not increase much. Monitor both domain-specific and general perplexity during fine-tuning to detect forgetting. Perplexity on the instruction template (prompt tokens) is also sometimes tracked to verify the model hasn't forgotten the chat format.

deepDiveIntro: Perplexity is the exponential of the average negative log probability that the model assigned to each token in the test sequence. It equals the exponentiated per-token cross-entropy. Intuitively, a perplexity of K means the model was as uncertain at each step as if it were choosing uniformly among K equally likely options — lower is better. [1]

Bits-per-character is an equivalent formulation that normalizes by characters rather than tokens, making it comparable across models with different tokenizers. Perplexity computed per token is not comparable across models with different vocabularies or tokenization schemes because the same text produces different numbers of tokens. When reporting or comparing perplexity, the tokenizer, test set, and normalization unit must all match. [1]

deepDive.heading: Perplexity Formula and Computation
deepDive.body: For a causal language model, perplexity is the exponential of the average cross-entropy loss on the test set. In PyTorch, calling the model with labels set to the input tokens returns this average loss directly; perplexity is then the exponential of that value. For texts longer than the model's context window, sliding-window evaluation scores only the latter portion of each window so that every token is conditioned on sufficient context. [1]

deepDive.heading: Tokenizer Dependence and Comparability
deepDive.body: Perplexity is normalized per token, not per word. A tokenizer that splits words into more subword tokens (BPE with smaller vocabulary) produces longer sequences → more prediction steps → perplexity measured on finer-grained tokens. This makes perplexity incomparable across models with different tokenizers. Example: GPT-2 (vocab=50K, BPE) and BERT (vocab=30K, WordPiece) would have different perplexities on the same text even if they had identical language modeling ability. Solution: report bits-per-character (BPC) which normalizes by characters — tokenizer-agnostic. [1]

deepDive.heading: Perplexity vs Downstream Task Performance
deepDive.body: Lower perplexity on held-out text generally correlates with better downstream task performance, but the correlation is imperfect. Scaling laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022): perplexity decreases as a smooth power law with training compute. Downstream task performance shows 'emergent' step-function improvements at certain perplexity thresholds. Implication: perplexity is a useful proxy during training but shouldn't replace task-specific evaluation. A model fine-tuned with RLHF may have higher perplexity (vs SFT baseline) on general text but much better instruction-following — perplexity and alignment can trade off. [1]

youtubeLabel: Perplexity in NLP — How Good Is Your Language Model?
takeaways: PP(W)=exp(−(1/N)·Σ log p(wₜ|w<t)) — exponentiated per-token cross-entropy. [1]
Interpretable as effective branching factor: PP=K means model as uncertain as uniform over K options. [1]
Only comparable across models with the same tokenizer and test set. [1]
BPC (bits-per-character) is tokenizer-agnostic — use for cross-model comparison. [1]
Lower perplexity ≈ better LM, but RLHF alignment can increase perplexity while improving helpfulness. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does perplexity increase after RLHF/SFT alignment?
edgeCases.a: RLHF and SFT shift the model distribution away from the base pre-training distribution — the model becomes more likely to generate instruction-following responses and less likely to generate arbitrary continuations. On a general held-out corpus (e.g., Wikipedia, web text), the base model has lower perplexity than the SFT/RLHF model because it was trained to predict any text. The alignment-trained model assigns lower probability to random completions and higher probability to instruction-following patterns. This increases perplexity on general benchmarks but improves helpfulness scores. Perplexity should only be compared on the distribution relevant to the model's intended use — not general web text for an instruction-tuned model.

edgeCases.q: How does sliding window perplexity differ from full-sequence perplexity?
edgeCases.a: Full-sequence perplexity: process the entire sequence in one forward pass (requires model context length ≥ sequence length). Sliding window: process the sequence in overlapping windows of max_length, scoring a stride-length suffix in each window. The key difference: in full-sequence evaluation, all tokens are conditioned on the same context (the full prefix). In sliding window, early tokens get less conditioning context. For models with max_length << text length, sliding window is necessary. The stride parameter trades off computation vs accuracy: stride=max_length (non-overlapping) is fastest but scores early tokens with no context; stride=1 (maximum overlap) is most accurate but requires T forward passes. stride=max_length/2 is a good default. HuggingFace's perplexity tutorial uses stride=512, max_length=1024.

sources.label: Brown et al. (2020) — Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3) arXiv:2005.14165
